data.title: Strathcona Resources: A Special Dividend Is Around The Corner
data.description:
Introduction

Strathcona Resources (SCR:CA) (OTCPK:STHRF) is a Canadian heavy oil producer with a current production rate in excess of 100,000 barrels of oil per day. The company recently "lost" out on its bid to acquire MEG Energy, but its shareholders will be rewarded with a C$10 special dividend per share.

A robust performance in Q3 despite a lower oil price

In the third quarter of the current financial year, the company produced approximately 116,200 barrels of oil-equivalent per day, with 99.6% of the total oil-equivalent production actually consisting of oil. There was a very small contribution from discontinued operations as Strathcona completed the sale of some of its assets in the first few days of the quarter.

The oil price was, of course, weaker than in the same quarter of last year, and Strathcona reported an average realized oil price of just under C$75 per barrel, which is almost 10% lower than the average realized price in the third quarter of 2024. This resulted in a total revenue of just over C$1B before taking royalties into consideration, and a net revenue of approximately C$903M, and this includes a C$27M loss on existing hedges.

As you can see in the income statement above, the company definitely remained profitable as it recorded a pre-tax profit of C$201M and a net profit of almost C$145M, which works out to C$0.68 per share. This also brought the 9M 2025 EPS to C$2.13 per share despite recording a C$85M hedge loss, partially compensated by a C$24M FX gain.

I always like to have a look at the cash flow statement as well, as that determines how much the company can spend on its investments. As you can see below, the reported operating cash flow was approximately C$289M, but this includes almost C$67M in working capital changes but excludes roughly C$10.5M in lease payments. This means the adjusted operating cash flow was approximately C$335M in the quarter.

The total capex was C$282M, which means Strathcona reported a net free cash flow of approximately C$52M, or around C$0.25 per share, for the quarter. While that sounds somewhat disappointing, keep in mind a substantial portion of the capex represents growth capex. Subsequent to the recent asset sales, the annual sustaining capex should be around C$550M (as shown below), which represents approximately C$140M per quarter.

Applying that number to the Q3 results, the underlying net free cash flow generated by Strathcona is approximately C$195M, representing C$0.90 per share.

Strathcona "lost" out on MEG Energy – or is it a winner?

Strathcona Resources really increased awareness about the company when it entered into the race to acquire MEG Energy after the latter entered into an agreement with Cenovus Energy (CVE) (CVE:CA) to be acquired. Strathcona acquired a stake on the open market and continued to buy stock in an attempt to block the deal by vocally opposing it, and this ultimately resulted in Cenovus having to increase its offer to get the deal sealed.

Some market commentators looked at the end result as "Strathcona failed to land MEG Energy," but I’m not so sure Strathcona is the loser in this story.

First of all, it's making money on its position in MEG Energy, and this, in combination with recent asset sales, will allow the company to pay a special dividend of C$10 per share. This means the company is keeping its word, as it has always said it would pay that special dividend if its bid for MEG Energy were to be unsuccessful.

Secondly, as part of the agreement to cease its resistance against the Cenovus offer, it entered into an asset purchase agreement with the latter, allowing it to purchase about 5,000 boe/day of production for just C$150M. This is a cheap deal, and it also sparked the interest of the regulator, which required MEG Energy to provide additional details on this side deal before the vote was allowed to happen.

In any case, Strathcona is now very cashed up after the asset sale, and an upcoming shareholders meeting will decide on a C$10/share special dividend. This will cost the company approximately C$2.14B and will be funded from existing cash on hand while drawing down more funds from its line of credit (which was repaid subsequent to the asset sales).

For now, the company’s guidance for 2026 remains unchanged as it expects to produce 115,000-125,000 boe/day while maintaining a C$1B capex spending plan. However, it will provide an update once its acquisition of the Cenovus assets closes in the next few weeks.

Investment Thesis

Strathcona still represents a good proxy to gain exposure to the oil price, and this could still warrant a "buy" rating, although investors are warned there may be some dividend-chasing money in the stock now. Given the tax regulations in my jurisdiction, I have sold my position in Strathcona, as capital gains enjoy a better tax regime than (special) dividends.

That being said, I will be looking to get back in the stock after its ex-dividend date (depending on where it is trading, of course), as my decision to sell is solely based on the fiscal implications.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Steven Madden: The Shoe Is On The Wrong Foot (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Back in February of this year, I decided to take a fresh look at Steven Madden (SHOO). From the time I had originally rated the company a ‘buy’ in September of 2022 until I subsequently downgraded it back in July of 2024, the stock outperformed the market, rising 52.5% compared to the 45% increase that the S&P 500 has achieved. But after such a monumental on up the hill, so to speak, I made the decision to downgrade it. Since my most recent article reaffirming it as a ‘hold’ candidate, however, the stock has underperformed the market, rising only 4.8% compared to the 13% rise that the S&P 500 has seen.

To be honest with you, I'm not surprised by this. Recent revenue figures provided by management have been promising. After a strong 2024 fiscal year, revenue continued decline through the first nine months of 2025. But profitability and cash flows have become problematic. From 2023 to 2024, these figures were mixed. But this year, profitability and cash flows are down across the board. I would be more optimistic if shares of the business were cheap on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar companies. By considering how the stock is priced, and factoring in the issues that it's facing right now, I think that downgrading it to a soft ‘sell’ is the right choice.

Talk about pain

I don't think it would be terribly useful to dig too much into the financial data from 2023 and 2024. But for context, in the chart above, you can see what the company reported for those windows of time. Revenue increased nicely, but profitability declined. We also saw a contraction in operating cash flow. But on the other hand, adjusted net income, adjusted operating cash flow, and EBITDA all improved. If we were just seeing more of the same for this year, I would probably consider upgrading it. Because using last year's figures, shares really are not that expensive. But unfortunately, the picture looks different now.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first nine months of 2025. Revenue for the company, a firm that focuses on marketing and selling fashion forward branded and private label footwear, accessories, and apparel, still managed to grow, climbing from $1.70 billion to $1.78 billion. This jump in sales was driven, according to management, by a strong showing when it came to its Direct-To-Consumer segment. Overall revenue skyrocketed from $374.2 million to $529.1 million. Management attributed this increase of 29.7% from an extra 41.4% surge coming from incremental revenue from brick-and-mortar stores, concessions, and digital businesses that the company picked up as a result of its acquisition of Kurt Geiger. This London-based footwear and accessories brand was purchased by the company in a deal valued at $390.5 million at closing, as well as $12.9 million of contingent consideration. Without this acquisition, revenue for this segment would have fallen. But that's not the only area where organic weakness was.

For instance, the Wholesale Footwear segment for the business reported a decline in revenue from $832 million to $782.8 million. Meanwhile, the Wholesale Accessories/Apparel segment reported a drop from $487.3 million to $459.8 million. Management blamed this top line pane on the impact of tariffs. Specifically, they said that their off price and mass merchant customers ended up not buying as much because of the higher costs that tariffs mean for the business.

Even though revenue for the company improved nicely, profitability took a heck of a hit. Net income plummeted from $134.6 million to $21.5 million. Some of this was because of transaction related costs, including $38.8 million of expenses on the compensation side associated with acquisition related sellers proceeds. But even on an adjusted basis, net income for the business dropped from $153 million to $86.6 million. Once again, tariffs, as well as the addition of the lower gross margin concessions associated with the purchase of Kurt Geiger, negatively affected the business. Operating cash flow ended up falling from $94.2 million to $67.6 million. Even if we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a substantial drop from $191.6 million down to $70.1 million. And finally, EBITDA for the company declined from $219.2 million to $68.4 million.

Management expects the aforementioned acquisition to be instrumental in pushing revenue up year over year in the final quarter of this year by between 27% and 30%. But in addition to this, the company stands to benefit from a normalization on the wholesale side even as management implements strategic pricing actions and sourcing initiatives aimed at improving its own gross profit margin. Some of the benefit the company sees should come from marketing efforts with Millennials and Gen Z aimed at fueling demand for its Steve Madden brand, especially when it comes to boots, dress shoes, loafers, and more. Expected earnings per share for the final quarter should be between $0.41 and $0.46, which would take adjusted net profits to $31 million at the midpoint. It is worth noting, however, that even with this adjusted net income would be below the $39.4 million the company reported the same time last year.

If we take the total estimate for adjusted net income this year $117.6 million, which is down drastically from the $192.4 million the company reported for last year, and if we take results that we have already seen so far this year, we would expect adjusted operating cash flow to be about $142.7 million and for EBITDA to come in at around $164 million. Using these estimates, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently priced. These multiples are rather lofty, though not necessarily in the overvalued range. Compared to other similar firms, however, the stock is certainly pricey. In the table below, I stacked it up against five such enterprises. Four of the five ended up being cheaper than it is on both a price to earnings basis and on a price to operating cash flow basis. Meanwhile, on an EV to EBITDA basis, our prospect ended up being the most expensive of the group.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Steven Madden 23.1 19.0 17.9
Wolverine World Wide (WWW) 16.2 8.9 15.8
Rocky Brands (RCKY) 10.7 7.2 7.9
Crocs (CROX) 26.2 5.8 5.5
Deckers Outdoor Corporation (DECK) 12.1 11.5 8.1
Birkenstock Holdings (BIRK) 21.8 20.3 12.2

Takeaway

Normally, I wouldn't take such a drastic action as downgrading Steven Madden in response to these numbers alone. However, I am also worried about the economy more broadly. I believe that the US is heading toward a recession, and between the decrease in consumer demand and the painful impact of tariffs that remain a big uncertainty, I would argue that downgrading the company to a soft ‘sell’ is the right move.

data.title: Nvidia: Cockroaches Will Survive A Bubble Pop
data.description:
Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) dropped its Q3 '26 results on an angsty market and got a nod of approval with the stock up about 5% in pre-market on the outlook for the January quarter, outpacing consensus expectations and whisper numbers for $65B versus $61.8B and $64B, respectively, plus a guide for gross margins to revisit the 75% level. Tech stocks are trading up on the results, and it looks like markets are in for the first breather this month.

Heading into this print, our eyes were on Blackwell, specifically the ramp of GB300. We are sniffing around the right place, with GB300 accounting for two-thirds of Blackwell revenue this quarter. For the next two quarters, it’s all about Blackwell with Hopper (including H20) making up an “insignificant” part of Q3 revenue; CEO Jensen Huang notes in the release that “Blackwell sales are off the charts, and cloud GPUs are sold out...”

Data center sales for the quarter grew 25% to $51.2B (ahead of expected $49.3B), with compute growing 27% sequentially to $43B while networking grew 13% to $8.2B. The bonus of the Blackwell Ultra ramp is that it boosts networking revenue; it is designed for massive-scale use using NVL (which means dual-GPU products that are connected via high-speed NVLink interconnect) and Ethernet and Infiniband solutions.

The results this quarter were good, but they looked great against looming fears of an AI bubble and circular financing. Q3 revenue grew 22% Q/Q to $57B, beating expectations for $55B while non-GAAP gross margins expanded 90 basis points to 73.6%. The stock has been trading down heading into print pretty much flat on the three-month chart, shown below, and roughly flat QTD. Panic was augmented by big short investor Michael Burry reporting puts on Nvidia and Palantir (PLTR), SoftBank (OTCPK:SFTBY) selling its Nvidia stake for $5.83B, and Peter Thiel selling his.

So, the results yesterday were a reminder of how much weight Nvidia holds on markets, plus it was a confirmation that while everything looks very messy, markets can rest assured that demand continues to outpace supply and Nvidia remains the winner of all the AI infrastructure CapEx spend heading into 2026.

We were worried about Nvidia’s Q4 outlook heading into Wednesday’s results. Our concern came from data points coming out of the AI server supply chain players: Super Micro Computer (SMCI), Wistron, Wiwynn, among others, reported higher Q/Q inventory levels, some Taiwanese ODMs reported softer October sales, and CoreWeave (CRWV) reported a disappointing FY25 outlook due to “temporary delays related to a third-party data center developer behind schedule.”

We put all these data points side by side, and given that CoreWeave is the first neocloud in line to get GB300, we expected this could point to potential production issues within the GB300 ramp. We didn’t see this thesis play out this quarter, but we are still watching for it. We think this could be an issue that shows up in Q4 earnings results if monthly sales reports continue to signal weakness, which would almost automatically signal an issue with the GB300 ramp. Any technical issues with GB300 aren’t out of the ordinary, given the higher density and complexity of the product line and the liquid-cooling requirements that come with it. Having said that, if we don’t see these technical issues resolved quickly, we believe this will negatively weigh on Nvidia’s Q4 results in February, big time. That’s the risk we’re watching.

We think Nvidia is aware of this potential issue and is addressing it. Digitimes reported on Monday morning that Nvidia is tightening its grip on the next-gen Vera Rubin cooling supply chain by consolidating assembly among three ODMs, likely to be Foxconn, Wistron, and Quanta. The reason we're seeing Nvidia race to consolidate its supply chain is that it's struggling to keep up with high yields for more complex product line-ups. According to vendors:

The company often pushes new products into mass production while designs are still changing. They also note that a new generation frequently appears before the previous platform achieves stable, full-run production yields.

This is probably showing up with the GB300 ramp now, and Nvidia wants to get ahead of the game in time for the Vera Rubin, which requires even more compute density and power consumption, which means liquid cooling. The current estimate is that liquid-cooling components per cabinet will double on the next-gen Vera Rubin. We don’t think the tech itself is flawed; instead, we think these are the growing pains of a tech transition, confronting extremely high expectations that leave little room for slip-ups.

Earnings call recap: Where’s China?

This quarter’s results and Q4 outlook, similar to last quarter, do not take into account any H20 sales to China. Collette Kress, Nvidia CFO, noted that:

While we were disappointed in the current state that prevents us from shipping more competitive data center compute products to China, we are committed to continued engagement with the U.S. and Chinese governments and will continue to advocate for America's ability to compete around the world.

We didn’t, however, get any questions on China, the +$50B market, during the Q&A section, and hence expectations for Nvidia’s China business continue to be tethered to geopolitical tensions. The fact that we didn’t see more China commentary suggests there is no new update, but, at the same time, the fact that we didn’t see the conversation about re-entering that market shut down is a positive in disguise. Our belief is that Nvidia’s next meaningful leg of outperformance will come from resuming its AI chip sales to China, both because it is the world’s second-largest AI market and because this would be the upside surprise the market isn’t expecting.

Last month, CEO Jensen Huang confirmed that “At the moment, we’re 100 per cent out of China.” Nvidia’s market share in China has dropped from 95% to 0%, with the greatest pushback to its resumption of sales coming from a combination of Beijing warning domestic players against purchasing Nvidia AI chips due to national security concerns and the Trump administration pushing back against allowing the watered-down Blackwell chip, B30A, to be sold to the nation.

The result of this is a rush among Chinese domestic players to fill Nvidia’s gap. Huawei is the face of this domestic push, unveiling its AI chip roadmap back in September. Alibaba (BABA) even came out with an AI chip, T-Head, that competes with H20. Cambricon Technologies, now labelled the “Nvidia of China,” reported that revenue grew 14x last quarter. The way Rachel Yeo put it:

[Cambricon’s growth is] one of the starkest signs yet of how China’s chipmakers are benefiting from a national drive to replace restricted Nvidia Corp. gear during a domestic AI development boom.

The pressure is on, and Beijing isn’t waiting for the U.S. to approve the sale of more advanced AI chips to China. So, why do we expect Nvidia to tap into this market again? The answer lies in the fundamentals: the performance gap between China’s AI chips and those of the rest of the world is widening and will continue to do so as AI moves to more advanced nodes next year, while China lags behind, stuck at 7nm due to the U.S. export controls.

We expect Beijing to cave as this gap widens, and the competitiveness of domestic solutions has a ticking clock on it. In a commentary with Bloomberg’s Ed Ludlow, Jensen emphasized that Nvidia’s share in China is zero repeatedly and so we think building up from 0% expectations for China will be the next big growth driver that could drive up Nvidia’s market cap to cross $5.5T potentially.

Bottom Line:

We continue to be bullish on Nvidia and expect the stock to be an outperformer in 2026. We believe Nvidia has the element of surprise working in its favor if it can get back into China. Nvidia is a survivor "cockroach," and so we see it as more resilient to the entire AI bubble and circular financing conversation. Because, at least for the short-term, tier 1 and hyperscaler players will continue to spend on speculative demand, and all roads of spend lead to more revenue for Nvidia.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Bitcoin: How Many Chairs Should You Be Sitting On?
data.description:
Michael Saylor, CEO of Strategy Inc. (MSTR), recently likened his company being all-in on Bitcoin to sitting on the one correct chair. His chair metaphor implied Bitcoin is the best and only asset worth investing in today.

Today, with Bitcoin (BTC-USD) undergoing a correction of more than 25% since its recent all time high, I am revising my coverage for this cryptocurrency. In my past work I have discussed my bull and bear cases for BTC, why Bitcoin can be a global reserve asset (but not a global means of payment), and how a USD crisis is impacting it.

In today’s article I will focus on what exposure investors should consider to Bitcoin in their portfolios. Using Saylor’s metaphor, how many “chairs” should they be sitting on? This, in my view, is a question readers may ask themselves during the current market correction. Especially if they entered a position in Bitcoin recently, in hope of short-term returns.

Quick summary of my thoughts and thesis on Bitcoin

Personally, I see Bitcoin as the most obvious asymmetric bet in the market at the moment: the possibility of entering a new global reserve asset before it fully matures into one.

Bitcoin has all the characteristics it needs to become a superior global reserve asset: durability, divisibility, fungibility, portability, verifiability, and, most importantly in my view, scarcity.

The real question is whether the world's institutions will actually adopt it as a global reserve asset. To assess whether that is or isn’t a likely scenario, my valuation model for Bitcoin includes three scenarios and two cases:

  • A bullish case, with Bitcoin maturing to match Gold in market cap, at roughly $1,000,000 per coin.

  • A bearish case, with Bitcoin remaining an “online casino”, meaning an asset whose value is only derived from its volatility and short-term trading. In this scenario, I assign Bitcoin a valuation of $100,000 per coin, roughly where it has been trading for the last months.

The table below summarizes the outcome

Let’s talk about exposure to Bitcoin

The exact amount of exposure to Bitcoin in one’s portfolio depends on personal objectives, attitude towards risk, and overall thoughts about Bitcoin.

Anyone who is not convinced of my thesis or of Bitcoin’s technical characteristics should not be exposed to Bitcoin at all, for example. Conversely, risk-tolerant investors that are very bullish on Bitcoin may be fine with a significant exposure to it, up to a majority of their portfolios.

Another important thing to consider is that some investors may find themselves holding a significant portion of their portfolios in Bitcoin, even if their cost basis is not as significant. That is my case: having bought Bitcoin at an average price of ~$25,000 per coin, I do have an exposure to BTC that, at market prices, reached up to over 30% of my overall portfolio when Bitcoin hit its ATH.

Ultimately, allocating Bitcoin in one’s portfolio is an exercise that balances the potential for superior returns with the risk of volatility. With that in mind, I want to provide some food for thought on what exposure to Bitcoin readers should consider, using a specific use case, with the below assumptions:

  • A 5-year buy-and-hold period.

  • A comparison of Bitcoin to an investment in the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO).

  • Return figures roughly based on the last 5 years’ performance: a +400% return for BTC and a +90% return for VOO (rounded for simplicity).

  • A “stress test,” assuming that after the above returns, Bitcoin crashes 60% (entering a bear market) while VOO crashes 15%.

  • Two exposure scenarios: Investor A being exposed 100% to Bitcoin and Investor B being exposed 20% to Bitcoin and 80% to VOO.

The table below summarizes the return of such an exercise.

Scenario

Investor

Allocation

Component Outcomes

Total Portfolio Value

Portfolio Return

Bull Case

Investor A

$100,000 BTC

BTC → $500,000

$500,000

+400%

Investor B

$20,000 BTC $80,000 VOO

BTC → $100,000

VOO → $152,000

$252,000

+152%

Stress Test

Investor A

$100,000 BTC

BTC → $40,000

$40,000

−60%

Investor B

$20,000 BTC $80,000 VOO

BTC →

$8,000

VOO → $68,000

$76,000

−24%

The result, in my view, indicates how some Bitcoin exposure can significantly boost returns (based on historical data) while not having too much of an impact on volatility. Being fully exposed to Bitcoin, on the other hand, provides significant volatility that would see an investor losing most of their initial capital in case of a sharp correction.

Fine-tuning Bitcoin exposure

After having determined that a complete exposure to Bitcoin is not recommendable for most, a more interesting exercise concerns different levels of exposure. Should investors consider only a minor exposure to Bitcoin? Or should they consider up to half of their portfolio in Bitcoin?

To answer those questions, I created the following two additional scenarios, which feature two new types of investors:

  • Investor C, exposed to Bitcoin for 50%

  • Investor D, with a minor exposure to Bitcoin of 5%

The other assumptions remain exactly the same as the previous exercise. Below are the results.

Scenario

Investor

Allocation

Component Outcomes

Total Portfolio Value

Portfolio Return

Bull Case

Investor C

$50,000 BTC $50,000 VOO

BTC → $250,000 VOO → $95,000

$345,000

+245%

Investor D

$5,000 BTC $95,000 VOO

BTC → $25,000 VOO → $180,500

$205,500

+105.5%

Stress Test

Investor C

$50,000 BTC $50,000 VOO

BTC → $20,000 VOO → $42,500

$62,500

−37.5%

Investor D

$5,000 BTC $95,000 VOO

BTC → $2,000 VOO → $80,750

$82,750

−17.3%

What this new scenario indicates, in my view, is that a minimal exposure of 5% to Bitcoin serves little or no purpose today. It doesn’t provide significant upside (Investor D’s performance is significantly lower than Investor B), but it still drags down overall performance during a bear market. In other words, with Bitcoin having already matured to a multi-trillion-dollar asset, future reasonable returns are not enough to justify a minor exposure.

Bitcoin bulls should consider an exposure, in my view, of at least 15%-20% of their portfolio in order to see some benefits. That, of course, still comes with significant risks, which I will cover next.

Risks

I already mentioned at the beginning of this article that an investment in Bitcoin only makes sense for those who are bullish on it. As obvious as this sounds, this is something worth repeating, as I often get Bitcoin skeptics debating me in my BTC pieces, arguing that Bitcoin will never work. My entire work on Bitcoin, including this article, is based on the assumption that Bitcoin has everything it takes to mature into a global reserve asset, and that this outcome is more likely than not to happen.

In that regard, the main risk of any type of exposure to Bitcoin is that this cryptocurrency may never mature to a global reserve asset and rather trade close to the current range indefinitely.

Another risk concerns the fact that, today, BTC and the S&P 500 are not perfectly correlated. Pairing them reduces overall portfolio volatility while still allowing participation in Bitcoin’s upside. However, this is also based on historical data and may change in the future. If Bitcoin were to mature to a global reserve asset, it could also acquire the relatively low volatility of gold, the world’s global reserve asset today.

Conclusion: No, Bitcoin should not be your “only chair”

Even just five years ago, an argument could be made that an exposure to Bitcoin of 1%–5% of one’s portfolio could be beneficial. Today, Bitcoin bulls will find very little benefit from such a small exposure, which is certain to drag down their performance during bear markets but provides little upside overall.

This said, a complete exposure to Bitcoin is not preferable either, as it exposes investors to significant capital depletion. In that sense, I do not share Saylor’s idea that a total exposure to Bitcoin is not only desirable, but even “obvious.” I rather believe Bitcoin bulls should be exposed between 15% and 30% to Bitcoin in their portfolio, depending on their attitude towards risk and tolerance to volatility.

My “BUY” rating of Bitcoin for this article reflects my continued, bullish take on Bitcoin and my own exposure to BTC, which reflects my final recommendation.

data.title: Novo Nordisk: Potential Winner With Value Trap Risks
data.description:
I previously covered Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO) (OTCPK:NONOF) in August 2025, discussing how the compounding/regulatory headwinds had triggered its stock price corrections, worsened by the prior market rotation to AI-related stocks.

With NVO's tailwinds from the upcoming oral candidate launch negated by the sudden management change, I had believed that stock's correction presented an excellent dollar cost averaging opportunity - resulting in my Buy rating then.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for NVO, thanks to the emergence of a double bottom indicator, the consistent bounce at the P/E support levels of 12x, and the recovery tailwinds arising from its oral GLP-1 candidate.

Otherwise, NVO also presents a potential value trap, attributed to the consistently lowered FY2025 guidance and numerous growth headwinds, with the stock only suitable for those with higher risk appetite.

NVO Presents A Mixed Investment Thesis

NVO 1Y Stock Price

Since my last Buy rating, NVO has notably underperformed the wider market and its GLP-1 therapy peers, partly attributed to the mixed FQ3'25 performance and the lowered FY2025 guidance for the fourth time.

This is on top of the growth headwinds arising from the drug price deals, the therapy's mixed efficacy rates, the GLP-1 price war, and the consistently lowered consensus forward estimates - all of which present its mixed near-term investment thesis.

1. Drug Price Deals

For one, I expect NVO to report a notably impacted top/bottom-line performance from FY2026 onwards, attributed to the drug-pricing deal across the "Medicare, Medicaid, as well as cash-paying customers, through a government-run website named TrumpRx."

This is a material development indeed, since NVO's Ozempic/Wegovy monthly prices are expected to be down notably from the prior range of "$1,000 and $1,350" to $245 for Medicare/Medicaid and $350 for TrumpRx.

Given that the US region comprises 62.6% of NVO's YTD Obesity care revenues, there is a great cause for concern indeed, as the management also hints at an impact of "a negative low single-digit impact on global sales growth for the full year of 2025" had the prices "been introduced first of January 2025."

While the Maximum Fair Price [MFP] for Medicare Part D is expected to be effective only from January 01, 2027 onwards, I am of the opinion that NVO's near-term performance is likely to underwhelm given the upcoming launch of TrumpRx in 2026.

2. Efficacy Rates

LLY's Expanding Market Share

This is especially since NVO's US incretin analogs market share has moderated to 41.7% in Q3'25 (-0.8 points QoQ/YoY data unavailable), as Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) continues to gain share and the latter's "Zepbound exited Q3 with 71% share of new prescriptions."

It is apparent from these developments that NVO's strategic partnership with CVS Health (CVS) since July 01, 2025 has not worked out as intended in driving renewed US growth prospects.

Part of the headwinds may attributed to NVO Wegovy's lower weight loss rate at -13.7% of the patients' body weight by week 72, compared to LLY Mounjaro's accelerated weight loss rate at -20% and Zepbound's at -20.2%.

Competition is heating up as well, with Viking Therapeutics' (VKTX) VK2735 delivering a weight loss rate of -14.7% by week 13 and Pfizer's (PFE) acquired pipeline from Metsera, MET-097i, also delivering an excellent average weight loss rate of -14.1% by week 28 and a maximum of 26.5% in select patients.

While both pipelines are still in the Clinical Phase 3 and 2, respectively, NVO will undeniably meet intense competition once these candidates are approved by the US FDA and launched commercially sometime near the end of the decade.

These reasons may be why NVO has previously started a bidding war for Metsera by late October 2025, albeit ultimately losing out to PFE attributed to the US Federal Trade Commission's opposition.

3. Price War

NVO's prospects have been further worsened by the growing compounding market in the US:

Novo Nordisk market research shows that compounding has continued to increase. Multiple entities continue to market and sell compounded GLP-1s and it is now estimated to be well above 1 million patients in the U.S. that are currently on compounded GLP-1. (Seeking Alpha)

At a time when NVO's GLP-1 prices are impacted, compounded GLP-1 offerings are likely to remain headwinds as well, as Hims & Hers Health (HIMS) "strategically reduced prices across compounded GLP-1 treatment plans by as much as 20%," with injectables now costing $199 monthly and oral therapies $69 monthly.

This has also led to NVO lowering the prices of the two lowest doses of Wegovy and Ozempic for an introductory period through March 2026, to match those currently offered by LLY - with it marking a potentially prolonged and painful price war.

With compounded GLP-1 offerings likely to remain popular attributed to their improved long-term affordability, I am of the opinion the NVO's prospects are likely to remain mixed moving forward.

This is especially since the cheaper prices (arising from Medicare/Medicaid/TrumpRx) are likely to set a new normal in GLP-1 therapy prices, with the price erosion already triggering a notably smaller anti-obesity global market size of $95B by 2030, down from the prior estimates of $130B.

Combined with the increased availability of GLP-1 therapies from numerous market players over the next few years, I am of the opinion that these offerings may eventually be less of a novelty and more of a commodity, with it likely eroding NVO's first market mover advantage.

4. NVO's Cheap Valuations May Be A Value Trap

The Consensus Forward Estimates

As a result, I can understand why the consensus have also lowered their forward estimates, with NVO expected to report impacted top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +5.3%/+3% through FY2027.

This is a direct contrast to LLY's consistently raised estimates at +24.7%/+44% over the same time period, with it exemplifying the market's extreme pessimism surrounding NVO's future prospects, against the latter's 5Y historical growth profile at +18.9%/+22.5%, respectively.

NVO Valuations

As a result of the underwhelming GLP-1 monetization trends and the consistently downgraded FY2025 guidance, it is unsurprising that NVO has been consistently downgraded to FWD EV/Sales valuations of 4.71x and FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 13.43x by the time of writing.

This is down notably from the 5Y mean of 10.38x/30.34x, the 10Y mean of 8.27x/24.41x, and the sector median of 3.82x/19.07x, respectively.

Based on the current Enterprise Value of $225.77B and the FY2030 revenue estimates of $66.09B, NVO appears rather discounted at my estimated FY2030 EV/Sales valuations of 3.41x as well, compared to its GLP-1 peers, including:

  • GLP-1 leader - LLY at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 14.98x/my estimated FY2030 EV/Sales valuations of 8.37x (using the same methodology),
  • GLP-1 candidate through Metsera acquisition, PFE at 3.03x/3.16x,
  • pre-revenue GLP-1 player - VKTX at NA/2.57x, respectively.

NVO Technical Indicators

On the other hand, NVO's technical indicators remain mixed at current levels, as the stock charts lower lows and lower highs despite losing -66.4% of its value since the June 2024 heights.

While I have observed the potential emergence of NVO's double bottom at the $45s by the time of writing, with it signaling a possible change in its future stock price momentum from the prior meltdown, it remains to be seen when sentiments may recover.

Author's Historical Rating

While I have repeatedly highlighted NVO's robust capital appreciation opportunity upon a reversal in its performance metrics and an upward re-rating in its valuations, it is also apparent that my prior Buy ratings have not worked out as expected.

Pending the emergence of its bullish support and/or further growth catalysts, readers may want to note the risks of NVO potentially being a value trap at current levels, with a breach of the false double bottom a possible outcome.

NVO's Reversal Hinges On Outsized Oral Therapy Outperformance

NVO 5Y Stock Price

With NVO effectively losing all of its 4Y gains, it remains to be seen if the double bottom may be sufficient to convince the bulls to finally defend a trading floor, attributed to its mixed prospects from the numerous headwinds discussed above.

I am of the opinion that its near-term recovery prospects hinge on NVO's ability to successfully market its oral obesity/diabetes candidate in 2026, namely the once-daily Wegovy upon the upcoming US FDA approval sometime by the end of 2025.

My optimism is attributed to NVO's oral Wegovy delivering an improved weight loss rate at -16.6% by week 64, compared to LLY's orforglipron at -10.5% by week 52, with it presenting a potential catalyst to the former's growth prospects, since most patients typically prefer oral delivery at 62% compared to injection at 16%.

This is especially since NVO has already forged numerous partnerships/channels to rapidly launch the oral GLP-1 therapy upon approval while "already started manufacturing the oral GLP-1 ahead of the FDA approval decision," with it implying a rapid commercialization cadence right from the start.

As a result of its first to market advantage and the improved weight loss results, I believe that NVO may very well capture a robust oral therapy market share moving forward, against Goldman Sachs Research's expectation of " daily oral pills will capture some 25% share of the anti-obesity medication market by the end of the decade."

These imply a potentially drastic reversal in NVO's prior underperformance, the consequent upgrading in its current P/E valuations of 13.43x nearer to the 5Y P/E mean of 30.34x (cheap compared to LLY at 43.39x), and the projected doubling in its stock prices beyond current levels.

While it may be hard to time the bottom, I am of the opinion that the stock offers a relatively compelling risk reward profile at current levels, aided by the consistent bounce at the P/E floor of 12x during the worst of the August/November 2025 sell-off.

This is on top of the overly priced in growth headwinds, the oral candidate tailwinds, the rich cash flow generation, and the growing book value per share at $38.23 (+41.2% YoY) - with a floor likely to emerge sooner than later.

I am reiterating my Buy rating for the NVO stock here, while similarly looking to initiate a small position near to the 52 weeks bottom of ~$45.

It goes without saying that NVO may be a value trap at current levels, as discussed above, with my reiterated Buy rating only suitable for those with a higher risk appetite and a longer investing trajectory.

data.title: Orion Group Holdings Stays (Barely) On The Watchlist
data.description:
Since Travis Boone took over as chief executive officer of Orion Group Holdings (ORN) in September 2022, Orion has proven that it is a good business. With a nearly 300% rally since then, however, the core question is whether ORN is a good stock.

There's a strong case that it is. Valuation is reasonable, particularly after a post-earnings rally late last month has reversed in recent weeks. A guidance hike following that earnings report supports near-term confidence, while political and secular tailwinds blow behind each of the company's two operating businesses.

Yet after owning ORN during the first two years of Boone's tenure, I can't quite get over the hump to buy back in. There is just enough in terms of illiquidity, volatility, and risk to keep me waiting for a still-better price — even if, admittedly, that price may not arrive.

The Bull Case For ORN Stock

I wrote following Q1 that after a successful turnaround under Boone, the hard part was arriving for Orion. Low-hanging fruit — notably the improvement poor execution and poor bidding in the Concrete segment — had largely been harvested. That alone suggested impressive bottom-line growth (EBITDA rose 142% between 2021 and 2024) would slow.

Even with ORN stock up 12% since then, the bull case is that Q2 and Q3 results suggest the hard part might not actually be that hard. The increased full-year guidance at the midpoint suggests 7%-plus year-over-year growth in Adjusted EBITDA, despite a few hundred basis points of pressure from increased SG&A. That SG&A spend is going to help capture future opportunities created by strong demand in both segments.

And those future opportunities look legitimate. In Marine, Orion has been shortlisted for a pair of billion-dollar contracts in the Pacific. The focus of the Trump Administration on deterrence in the region likely suggests continued government demand. Closer to home, activity continues to pick up around dredging and port and bridge construction, thanks in part to past infrastructure authorizations being fulfilled.

In Concrete, Orion is a lower-order play on generative artificial intelligence, as it's built out a solid business constructing data centers. Per the Q3 call, the company now has delivered 39 such projects; that end market accounts for ~27% of revenue and the pipeline for the segment (and thus ~10% for Orion as a whole). Costco (COST) has become a significant customer in concrete as well as it continues to expand.

One of Boone's first tasks was fixing margins in the business — Orion had developed a bad habit of underbidding on projects, which pushed segment margins into the red — and, even with what is hopefully a blip so far this year, progress there continues. As management noted after Q2, segment reporting includes allocated corporate costs; excluding those expenses, Concrete margins are running at ~5%, and there's room for several hundred basis points of expansion going forward.

In other words, this should be a growing business on the top and bottom lines. The external environment is favorable and management seems to have proven itself. Yet ORN stock isn't priced for much growth. With a pullback erasing most of a two-session, 30% post-earnings spike, shares trade at less than 8x the midpoint of Adjusted EBITDA guidance for the year ($45 million). Following the long-awaited sale of a property in Houston (emphasis on 'long'; it was first listed in 2020 and first went under contract the following year), Orion actually has about $1 million in net cash, and EV/EBITDA is under 8x.

Normalized free cash flow should be in the range of $25 million (management has said in the past that maintenance capex runs at ~$15M), suggesting P/FCF of about 14x. Add 200 bps of margin expansion in Concrete and even mid-single-digit growth on the overall top line and that multiple gets closer to 11x-12x; basically, a zero-growth figure. In that context, the initial post-earnings price above $11 seems to make much more sense than Wednesday's close at barely $9. That in turn suggests that the simple status quo implies ~20% gains in Orion stock — with potential further upside from an acceleration in data center, government contract wins, or other factors.

Zeroing In On Margins

One core question for ORN, however, is that while those headline multiples 'feel' cheap, it's not clear that they're actually that far out of line. Peer Great Lakes Dredge & Dock (GLDD) trades at a mid-7x EV/EBITDA multiple. Great Lakes has more debt, and Orion's Pacific exposure might suggest something of a premium. But this probably just isn't a business that is going to consistently trade at or even near 10x EBITDA. P/FCF perhaps has some room for expansion (and GLDD is a difficult peer given how lumpy its cash generation historically has been), but it's not hard to wonder if Marine can drive much in the way of multiple expansion.

The problem seems even larger on the Concrete side. It is good news in context that segment contribution margins are in the 5% range. But that also means that segment contribution margins are in the 5% range — hardly a compelling number. And while management has said the business is on track, those margins are down sharply so far this year: nearly ten full percentage points.

Some of that pressure does come from higher corporate costs, admittedly, which (again) management has explained as spending meant to position Orion for growth going forward. Still, underlying margins have taken a step back, and that raises the concern of whether the march to high-single-digit contribution margins (at least) actually are achievable. On the Q2 call, management noted "increased competition" in the data center space, and while Orion likely can hold its own that competition may well put a cap on pricing. It's not hard to imagine rivals looking to do work for minimal, if any, profit, in a bid to gain position in what should be a fast-growing end market.

The Concrete Question

To be sure, history suggests there is still some room for margin expansion:

Marine is not quite where it peaked late last decade, and Concrete has returned to the negative levels driven by poor execution. As investments this year bear fruit and/or fall off, Orion should be able to get back to the 6-7% range on a consolidated basis, against a likely low-5% print this year.

That in turn adds 20% at least to EBITDA; top line growth can drive further increases. Even without multiple expansion, on paper an investor can pretty easily model double-digit returns here; again, the fundamentals suggest an ORN stock price closer to $11 (or better) than the current $9.

All that said, it's not hard to wonder if the market is really going to consistently pay up for this business, particularly if Concrete doesn't show a quick rebound in terms of profits. Margins in both businesses are not particularly impressive. There are competitive and cyclical concerns here; political risk might even become an issue if a new administration is less aggressive in the Pacific. Existing contracts already are sliding to the right (obviously, the shutdown is a factor here); that's potentially good news for 2026, but it's worth noting that management said in 2024 that contracts were moving into this year. A huge miss in Q4 relative to consensus and to guidance was chalked up to the timing of projects; that in turn colors this year's revenue growth (the Q4 miss was ~6% of expected full-year revenue).

There is just enough here to have some patience, particularly with a stock that this year alone has seen multiple declines to lower, more attractive, levels. The easy bull case may well work, but a closer look suggests some reason for caution. That seems to be the market's takeaway from Q3, and at the very least there's no need to rush in until Orion gets another chance to change the market's mind.

data.title: Alphabet: Five Reasons To Buy The Shares
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I reiterate the recommendation to buy Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) shares. This article is the continuation of my coverage initiation thesis published on December 12, 2024. It is also important to highlight that the company is part of my recommended stock portfolio for 2025. The company's shares are one of the highlights of the year so far, and in this article, my intention is to give five strong arguments for the investor to buy the company's shares, and suppose what Berkshire would have done invest in the company.

Context

In the first week of the year I wrote an exclusive article for Seeking Alpha offering investors my recommended portfolio of stocks, and since then, the performance has been a success. Year to date the portfolio rises 24.5% while the SPY ETF rises 14.6%.

One of the holdings in the portfolio was the shares of Alphabet , due to its exposure to AI, quality of management and mainly, the discount compared to the others magnificent seven. Since then, the shares have risen 46%, and I'll show you why I remain confident in the investment thesis.

First Argument

I don't know if you realized this, but when we talk about artificial intelligence, Alphabet is the only large company that is vertically integrated. We can see below that we have companies working on applications, language models, cloud, and hardware. But only the owner of Google operates in all segments.

Becoming vertically integrated presents challenges, without a doubt, however, management has already demonstrated its ability to operate different businesses. Additionally, verticalization will bring gains in efficiency, synergy, and bring resilience to the operation, which I consider the first major reason for investors to have Alphabet in their portfolio.

Second Argument

The second argument has already been mentioned in previous articles, and is related to OpenAI and ChatGPT . Many analysts suggested that ChatGPT would kill Google's search engine. From the beginning I disagreed with this thesis, however, now I can refute it with authority through the graph below.

Data shows that the search engine still has more than 5 trillion searches per year, and revenue continues to grow despite the launch of ChatGPT. Although ChatGPT has changed the market balance, the second argument for investors to have Alphabet shares in their portfolio is its moat in the search engine.

Third Argument

Every week I publish an article about the American economy where I argue that we are not in an AI bubble, but even if we were in a scenario minimally similar to a bubble, Alphabet would be one of the best options to have in the portfolio.

As we can see, the company has one of the most robust cash reserves with $98 billion, behind only Microsoft and Berkshire Hathway , which would already be a great argument for investors to include Alphabet shares in their portfolio.

The most curious thing is that many analysts use Berkshire Hathaway's cash as one of the arguments to say that we are in a bubble, and close to a market crash. However, this argument seems to have gone down the drain with recent news. Despite rumors of an AI bubble, Berkshire Hathaway announced on November 14 that it acquired 17.8 million shares of Alphabet , in an investment of $4.3 billion.

Fourth Argument

Despite the recent appreciation of shares, the fourth argument is valuation. I argue that company shares will have multiples repriced due to the productivity gains that AI will bring, and also due to the interest rate cuts process. Therefore, my target for the company's P/E is an ATH of 33.5x earnings.

In this scenario, the company's shares trade at 27x projected earnings, compared to the target of 33.5x earnings, we have an upside of 24%, which corroborates my recommendation to buy the shares. Given my argument about valuation, I feel confident with the recommendation to buy when I see the grades of growth,profitability, momentum and revisions.

Another incredible analysis is how the company manages to beat increasingly optimistic market estimates. Since the first quarter of 2023, the company has beaten market estimates, with a single exception in revenues for the fourth quarter of 2024.

The company surprised the market in its latest results with robust growth in the search adverstising , Youtube , and in the promising cloud segment. Honestly, after listing all these arguments, it is difficult not to be optimistic about the investment thesis, in any case, let's know the risks to the thesis.

Fifth Argument

In my opinion, the most powerful argument, which should have motivated Berkshire Hathway to invest in the company, is exposure to the quantum computing segment. Overall, McKinsey projects that the quantum technology market could reach $198 billion by 2040.

It is a technology to solve problems that are unsolvable today, such as creating more efficient batteries, modeling financial risks with thousands of assumptions and variables, simulating molecules, accelerating the discovery of new medicines and the advent of AI.

Recently the company announced the Willow chip and the Quantum Echoes algorithm. Both positioned Alphabet very well in a market with great competition from technology companies and even banks.

Therefore, in my opinion Alphabet is extremely valuable for all the businesses it already has, but this embryonic quantum computing business could be one of the biggest markets of the future.

Potential Threats To The Thesis

We have known risks that have already been addressed in other articles, such as antitrust issues, regulatory challenges, competition in the search engine market, retention of talent in AI.

Not to mention that shares have already risen more than 45% in the year, meaning the risk-return ratio is no longer as attractive. But in this article I want to delve deeper into a risk that may be new to many investors. As we know, companies like Alphabet, Amazon , Meta and others are making strong investments in AI.

It recently caught my attention that Meta moved $30 billion in debt related to artificial intelligence off its financial balance sheet through special purpose vehicles ( SPVs ) , many analysts saw this as a financial engineering maneuver reminiscent of the 2008 crisis. In a simplified way, through this strategy Meta achieves increase debt and maintain stable leverage.

But of course, we are talking about Alphabet, not Meta. Well, recently Google came in as a guarantor for a data center expansion project by the bitcoin mining company TeraWulf worth $3 billion. We know that a lot of investments will be necessary, but it seems to me to be a strategy very similar to Meta, and I will analyze the company's results with even more caution to identify businesses of this type.

The Bottom Line

I started writing articles for Seeking Alpha in 2024, and with the aim of delivering even more value to the reader, I listed the stocks that would be part of my recommended stock portfolio for 2025. Alphabet shares were included in this portfolio. The year is coming to an end, and the company's shares are the main highlight of portfolio.

The verticalized AI business model provides resilience to the operation, and in parallel, ChatGPT was unable to displace Google's search engine. Additionally, the company has robust cash flow if it goes through adverse times, and the earnings momentum is very strong.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate my recommendation to buy the company's shares. In my opinion, the risk-return ratio remains very attractive. In my calculations, we still have a robust upside of 24% to invest in the company's shares.

data.title: Amazon: OpenAI Deal Is A Game Changer
data.description:
Business is booming for Amazon (AMZN). The company’s third-quarter earnings on October 30th painted a clear picture of strength across the board, led once again by its crown jewel, Amazon Web Services (AWS). The results were met with enthusiasm from investors, pushing the stock to new all-time highs.

In Q325, Amazon crushed it in cloud, with the business growing more quickly than in the prior quarter, proving investors wrong that cast doubt on the company’s AWS growth. The use of artificial intelligence is starting to have an enterprise-wide effect on the company as Amazon is growing more quickly, which in turn is driving significant earnings growth. Shares hit a record high following Q325 earnings, yet my view on Amazon has never been more bullish.

Growth and Margin Upside for Years to Come

Amazon’s Q3 2025 results came in ahead of expectations, fueled by steady retail sales and a meaningful reacceleration in AWS. Every segment, from domestic and international e-commerce to cloud services, delivered at least 11% year-over-year growth. The U.S. retail business alone generated more than $106 billion in turnover during the quarter, continuing to anchor Amazon’s massive top line.

The real standout, though, was AWS. Segment revenue climbed 20% year-over-year to $33.0 billion, up from 17.5% growth in the prior quarter. That improvement might sound small, but it’s a significant swing given AWS’s scale, and it reaffirms that demand for Amazon’s AI and cloud offerings remains strong. Total company revenue grew 13% year-over-year, proving that AWS continues to act as Amazon’s main growth engine.

After years of prioritizing reinvestment, Amazon is finally hitting its stride in profitability. Third-quarter net income reached $21.2 billion, or roughly $230 million in profit per day. On an annualized basis, that’s about $84 billion in earnings power over the next twelve months, potentially more if AWS keeps accelerating.

No analysis of Amazon would be complete without shining a light on the AWS segment, which is the primary growth driver for the company. In the third quarter, AWS earned $11.4 billion from selling services on its cloud platform, mostly to corporations like SAP (SAP), ServiceNow (NOW), or Qantas Airways (OTCPK:QABSY). Amazon’s cloud platform is still the most sought after in the market, which should translate to years of sustainable net revenue growth. Per App Economy Insights, AWS has a significant lead in the cloud infrastructure market with a 29% market take-rate, leading Microsoft (MSFT) by 9 percentage points.

Profitability Momentum in the Cloud

AI-driven demand, both from enterprise and consumer clients, continues to fuel AWS’s profitability. One of the best metrics to gauge this is operating income margin (OIM), calculated as operating income divided by net sales. In Q3, AWS’s OIM rose to 34.5%, up from 32.9% the previous quarter.

That margin expansion tells the story: AWS is not only growing revenue but also operating more efficiently. Amazon’s total operating income climbed 10% year-over-year to $11.4 billion, showing how the cloud segment’s performance is lifting the company overall.

As growth catalysts for Amazon, I would point to the company’s AWS acceleration momentum, higher operating income, a rebound in operating income margins in Q325, and the company’s long-term potential in the CSP market, catalyzed by higher capex in the industry as a whole.

AWS/OpenAI Deal Is Set To Accelerate Amazon’s Cloud Growth

AWS announced a $38 billion deal for cloud services with OpenAI earlier this month. This deal, which will see its contract value get spread out over a seven-year period, will give OpenAI access to Amazon’s GB200 and GB300 chips that are deployed in the company’s Amazon EC2 UltraServers. OpenAI is taking a major step forward here in the diversification of its revenue profile, as the company has previously been reliant on Microsoft for its cloud infrastructure. But the deal is obviously much more significant for Amazon.

First, the deal sets the stage for a revenue acceleration for AWS. The cloud services agreement will see a ramp-up period until the end of 2026, which is when Amazon will be in a stage of full-capacity deployment. This means that AWS could grow even faster than it is now and become a LOT more profitable in the future.

The OpenAI contract, worth an average of $5.4 billion per year, will not only accelerate AWS growth but also hand the company a strategic win over its peers, particularly Azure and Google Cloud Platform. In 2024, AWS generated $39.8 billion in operating income, which I see developing to more than $186 billion by 2029, using growth rates of 35% (2026) and then using a sliding percentage schedule.

Over the horizon of my projection period (2025-2029), Amazon’s growth could yield a more than 3.2x increase in net revenue and a 4.7x increase in operating income. I expect to see a significant increase in operating margins over time as AWS benefits as a leading CSP from growing cloud compute and chip demand. This should fundamentally benefit the company’s margins as it expands its international data center footprint. I am particularly optimistic about AWS given the growing size of the server market, which underpins a bullish sector outlook more broadly. The projections below are based on the idea that Amazon will be able to grow its net revenue at a quicker pace in the future than in the past, with the OpenAI contract providing a catalyst.

What’s more, AWS is already profitable to a serious extent, which makes cloud platform-derived growth so much more valuable than revenue growth in e-commerce. In my calculation below, I highlight the estimated trajectory of AWS revenue, though I plan with a decelerating growth rate after 2027 (to be conservative). As such, my net revenue growth rate in the AWS segment will fall slowly over time to about 20% in 2029. The most important insight here is that a higher revenue base in the cloud segment and growing operating income margins are set to ignite an explosion in operating income growth for AWS, which may in the future even lead to a major share buyback.

It is entirely within the realms of possibility, in my estimation, for Amazon’s cloud platform to grow to a near $200 billion income base at the end of my five-year planning horizon in 2029. To achieve this, Amazon can sign new (high-margin) AWS partnership deals with major corporations, expand geographically to high-growth regions such as the Asian-Pacific, and sell more custom-designed chips (like Trainium and Inferentia), which are optimized for AI/ML workloads.

Amazon’s Trainium2 chip revenue rose 150% YoY growth in Q325, with the business of producing custom-designed chips offering a long-term growth runway for AWS, thanks to growing confidence in capex spending. Trainium chip sales in particular could be a source of cloud strength for AWS in the future and drive interest in the company’s shares.

Valuation: Amazon’s Cloud Potential Isn’t Fully Priced In

Amazon reached a record high after Q325 as investors cheered the company’s business performance, and its cloud performance led to particular praise. With Q425 likely also shaping up to be a good quarter, at least for the e-commerce part of the company’s business, I see upside for Amazon’s shares.

Amazon’s shares go for 31.4x FWD earnings vs. 25.1x for Alphabet (GOOG) and 30.9x for Microsoft, all of which compete in the same cloud market. I used consensus estimates from SA to derive FWD P/S and P/E ratios for the three CSPs below. Microsoft seems to be particularly expensive based on net revenue (P/S of 11.3x), while Amazon is actually the CSP with the lowest net revenue multiple (P/S of 3.3x). The market clearly assigns a premium to Microsoft’s Azure growth story, but I’d argue Amazon deserves similar treatment. AWS has nearly identical growth potential, if not greater, given its scale and leading market share. If Amazon’s valuation were to move closer to Alphabet’s P/S of 7.4x, the upside could be substantial.

With an estimated 2026 revenue of $792 billion, Amazon remains the largest cloud and e-commerce player in the world. Based on a fair forward P/S multiple of 4.0x to 5.0x, I arrive at an equity fair value range between $3.2 trillion and $4.0 trillion, or roughly $296 to $370 per share. Compared to the current share price of $244, that suggests potential upside of 21% to 52%. Amazon’s share price as of the time of writing this article is $222, so shares trade a cool 25% below my low-end fair value. The discount, as far as I can tell, is due to the market underestimating the scaling of the cloud footprint as well as the magnitude of the operating earnings ramp that is likely to transpire in the next five years.

Moreover, the large market share of approximately 30% is a major asset for Amazon, and the OpenAI backing creates a strong lever for accelerating net revenue growth.

Risks to Watch

Large and small U.S. businesses are spending big bucks on AI to evolve and enhance their value propositions for their own customers. This growth comes primarily from the conviction that AI will deliver productivity benefits and lower costs for corporations, leading to higher returns on invested capital.

Recently, however, concerns have mounted in the market about a potential AI bubble, which led to dropping market valuations last week, particularly in the ‘Magnificent 7’ group, after Big Short investor Michael Burry disclosed a short position in Palantir Technologies (PLTR) and NVIDIA (NVDA).

If AI bubble concerns prove to be accurate, the market may be in for a rough ride and a loss of confidence in CSP’s net revenue growth potential.

My Verdict

In summary, Amazon’s growth is not showing any signs of slowing down, and capital expenditures are expected to rise drastically going forward, which kind of bodes well for AWS, Amazon’s locomotive for growth. In the third quarter, the AWS segment grew to $33.0 billion in net revenue, which makes AWS easily a $130 billion annual business, and its growth is picking up steam.

With AI and cloud services growing, AWS is in a robust position to leverage its cloud services platform, a platform that is still leading the CSP market by a decent amount. The AWS/OpenAI deal is a game changer, in my opinion, and points to a growth acceleration for Amazon. Serious margin and operating income gains await investors.

With strong earnings momentum, a clear lead in cloud infrastructure, and attractive valuation multiples, Amazon remains my top growth pick for the long term.

data.title: PFN: Has Done Well This Year, Double-Digit Yield
data.description:
Thesis

The PIMCO Income Strategy Fund II (PFN) is a closed end fund that we own and last covered in the beginning of the year. In that piece we upgraded the fund and outlined its conservative stance in a tough environment for credit.

In today's article we are going to revisit the CEF and provide an analysis of its performance in 2025, as well as outline its forward given the current macro forces.

A robust 2025

Fixed income closed end funds are supposed to be leveraged approaches for traditional high yield. This means they tend to outperform both on the upside and on the downside. Versus a staple like the State Street SPDR Bloomberg High Yield Bond ETF (JNK), PFN has done well throughout the years:

We are big believers in visual representations, and the above table is quite good at highlighting the performances for the two names. As we can see from the above, PFN has historically been 1.5x the return for JNK, with a couple of outliers (2024 was a very good year for PFN against JNK, while 2020 was not).

PFN currently sports a 15% leverage ratio and also comes with an active management approach. This means that PFN can pivot towards corners of the fixed income market where undervaluation exists, while JNK sticks to its passive mandate. Let us see the current CEF composition.

Current fund composition

By looking on the CEF's webpage we can see its current asset allocation:

  • High yield credit: 39.5%
  • Non Usd developed: 16%
  • EM: 13.2%
  • Non Agency Mortgage: 10%
  • US Government Related: 9.5%
  • Invest. Grade Credit: 6.5%
  • CMBS: 3%
  • Other: residual

From a pure composition perspective the CEF is overweight U.S high yield, followed by developed markets bonds and EM debt. The portfolio duration is 3.5 years, and the CEF gives investors a 'duration weighted exposure' column, basically outlining where duration lies within the portfolio. For example the US Government related bucket has a 38% duration weighted fund exposure. This simply means the CEF holds treasuries that have a longer duration than the 3.5 years for the portfolio. Conversely the HY credit bucket is just 15% of the portfolio duration weighted, telling us the credits held there have a very short duration profile.

This set-up is good, because it limits the downside moves when credit spreads will blow higher. Furthermore if treasuries rally during a credit crisis the fund is set to benefit. There is therefore an embedded hedge in the portfolio here.

The fund does not disclose its ratings matrix, but given the asset buckets above we can guesstimate that only a very small proportion of the CEF is rated investment grade (namely the IG credit bucket, the treasuries bucket and potentially non agency mortgages). Our rough estimate is for a 25% to 30% allocation to IG credits here currently.

Volatility metrics and other analytics

Being a CEF with leverage, the fund is more volatile than a simple ETF like JNK. Morningstar gives us a 6% standard deviation (3-year) and a 1.39 Sharpe ratio. Let us see some other analytics here:

  • AUM: $0.7 bil
  • Distribution: 11.7%
  • Premium to NAV: 2.6%
  • z-stat: -1.4

We recently saw a move down in premiums on all of the Pimco CEFs, with this fund affected as well:

The CEF is trading handily within its historic 0% to 8% range, thus making the name fairly valued in our opinion when using this valuation metric.

Distribution is not covered

Given the low leverage ratio and tight spreads in the market, one could have guessed that the 11.7% distribution is not entirely covered:

As of a recent Section 19a notice, we can observe a 23% utilization of 'paid-in surplus' or return of capital. So the real cash flow backed distribution is closer to 9%, which is much more in-line with the total return which the fund is set to make this year.

From our view-point we understand the marketing aspect of having a high distribution, and are fine with ROC figures below 30%. We appreciate the fact that the fund has moved down the leverage spectrum and would rather see a lower distribution than a high leverage ratio.

Our only issue with the CEF is a macro one - tight spreads

At this juncture of the macro cycle, we view high-yield corporate credit as significantly overextended:

If we look at the ICE BofA Single-B US High Yield Index Option Adjusted Spreads, we can see they are at the bottom of the 10-year historic range. This simply means the next move is going to be up. We do not know when, but we would not want to buy HY here. Wait for a widening in spreads.

As we saw from our historic analysis, PFN will usually give a 1.5x move in respect to JNK. If JNK goes down by -10%, expect a -15% move in PFN. Thus a blow out in spreads will see a significant drawdown here. As a reference point, the fund had a -9% drawdown earlier in April 2025 when the market switched to a risk-off mode.

Conclusion

PFN is a fixed income closed end fund from Pimco. The CEF usually provides for a 1.5x return versus JNK, and has done very well in 2025, being close to a +10% total return for the year. The name has reduced its leverage this year, but will not escape a risk-off environment. We find the name fit for purpose, with no unnecessary risk factors. We like what the CEF is doing and will continue to hold, although we are most certainly concerned about tight credit spreads.

data.title: Nvidia: Bears Got This One Chart All Wrong (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) truly delivered a stellar quarter, showing a reacceleration in the Data Center segment, and setting an overall very bullish tone in the earnings call.

The stock is rightly up after these earnings, but bears are still trying their best to defend their position.

The latest “red flag” is the inventory growth, which some are labelling as channel stuffing.

However, upon further inspection, it seems to me like inventory isn’t out of proportion given sales growth and revenues.

This is not a Cisco (CSCO) of 1999 or an overvalued dot-com era company.

Having said this, investors should be wary about the true limitations and bottlenecks that Nvidia faces.

I am upgrading to a Buy rating.

Nvidia Q3

In my last post, I highlighted three risk factors going into Q3 earnings:

  • China.

  • AI bubble.

  • Competition.

At least on two of these points, I am now more confident after the Q3 earnings.

First off, Data Center revenue accelerated QoQ, up over 24%.

This is very encouraging, and paired with some of the comments from the earnings call, has eased my concerns of an AI bubble.

Salesforce's engineering team has seen at least 30% productivity increase in new co-development after adopting Cursor.

Source: Earnings Call.

And that’s not the only example that was given. AI is having tangible effects in businesses, something which I also highlighted in my last Palantir (PLTR) article.

Blackwell sales are off the charts, and cloud GPUs are sold out… we’ve entered the virtuous cycle of AI.

Source: Earnings Call.

It certainly doesn’t feel in the numbers like AI is slowing down. And even though I still believe competitors like Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) are putting up a good fight, there’s still a very healthy demand for Nvidia’s GPUs.

What I was right about, of course, was the issues with China.

H20 sales were approximately $50 million, sizable purchase orders never materialized in the quarter due to geopolitical issues and the increasingly competitive market in China.

Source: Earnings Call.

Basically negligible revenues in China, and the company is still not including any potential China revenues in their forward guidance.

And in terms of the margins, it does seem like Nvidia’s margins are near their upper bound, and it might be difficult to squeeze further out.

But overall, a great quarter which supports the bullish case.

The “Red Flag”

Nonetheless, there was one, on the surface, interesting point that bears made after this report.

Nvidia inventories have climbed through the roof over the last few quarters.

If demand is so high, why is there so much inventory?

Indeed, this could be a way of making revenues look better, but I don’t see that as the case at all.

If anything, high inventory can be explained by the fact that there is a huge expected demand.

Management has already guided towards $0.5 trillion in Blackwell and Rubin revenue.

This is massive.

On the other hand, consider the fact that inventory relative to revenues sits near very normal levels.

You’d expect inventory to grow as fast as revenues and sales. There’s nothing strange about this in my opinion, and this has been the way Nvidia has operated in the past, too.

In short, the "high" inventory isn't a sign of weakness; it's a buffer against even higher anticipated demand, helping Nvidia maintain its near-monopoly in AI chips while minimizing stockouts that could cede market share to rivals like AMD or custom silicon from cloud giants.

Other Considerations

This doesn’t mean there aren’t reasons for concern in the future.

The lack of China revenues is still an issue, and what’s more concerning is the geopolitical tension, which could hamper Nvidia’s supply capacity.

But perhaps the most glaring risk is that the AI rollout could soon run into an energy bottleneck.

Or rather, the current power grid could prove insufficient to accommodate all the new data center buildout.

This is certainly something investors should watch out for.

Final Thoughts

All in all, this was an outstanding quarter for Nvidia. It proved that it remains the king of GPUs and that concerns of an AI bubble are overplayed, at least for now.

Nvidia is still a solid Buy. I did say I would buy a dip after earnings. Well, we did get a dip, though it was before earnings, and now it seems like Nvidia will soon be making new highs.
